Figure 8: Example of generated test for an artificial API in which a SQL Injection vulnerability is detected.
Figure 2: Example of generated test for an artificial API in which a leakage information fault is correctly identified.

Figure 10: Snippet example of generated test case in which a Not Recognized Authentication (Section 3.1) fault is identified in familie-ba-sak. Due to reason of space, the generated test’s JavaDoc is not shown.
Figure 4: Example of generated test for an artificial API in which a PUT was successful without any authentication info.

Figure 7: Example of generated test for an artificial API in which first a call is made via OPTIONS to determine which methods are allowed, followed by a successful call to a GET that is not defined in the schema.

Table 2: Statistics of the employed 36 REST APIs in our empirical study for white-box testing, including number of source files, numbers of lines of code (LOCs), the number of HTTP endpoints, whether they require authentication, and the databases they use (if any).

Figure 6: Excerpt of a generated test for an artificial API in which an exception stack-trace is present in the response body. Due to long stack-trace (56 lines), the test is not displayed in its entirety.
